gold_final_answer: Either the multiset contains at least four zeros; or all numbers have the same nonzero absolute value and the number of negative numbers k satisfies k not in {1, 2, 2012, 2013}.
topics_flat: Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Factorization techniques
Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Sums and products
prompt: Find all sets of 2014 rational numbers not necessarily distinct such that: If an arbitrary number in the set is removed, one always can divide the remaining 2013 numbers into three sets such that each set has exactly 671 elements and the products of all elements in each set are equal.
response_text:
Let the multiset be
\[
A=\{a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{2014}\},
\]
where the \(a_i\) are rational numbers, not necessarily distinct.

We are told:

> For every choice of one removed element \(a_k\), the remaining \(2013\) numbers can be partitioned into three groups of \(671\) numbers each, such that the product of the numbers in each group is the same.

We must determine all such multisets.

---

## Claim
The only possible multisets are those in which all 2014 numbers are equal.

We now prove this.

---

## Step 1: Set up the condition algebraically

Fix \(k\). After removing \(a_k\), the remaining \(2013\) numbers can be split into three groups, each of size \(671\), with equal product. Let that common product be \(P_k\).

Then the product of all remaining \(2013\) numbers is
\[
P_k^3.
\]
So for every \(k\),
\[
\prod_{i\ne k} a_i
\]
is a cube in \(\mathbb{Q}\).

Let
\[
T=\prod_{i=1}^{2014} a_i.
\]
Then for each \(k\),
\[
\frac{T}{a_k}
\]
is a cube in \(\mathbb{Q}\).

Hence for any two indices \(i,j\),
\[
\frac{T/a_i}{T/a_j}=\frac{a_j}{a_i}
\]
is a ratio of two cubes, hence itself a cube in \(\mathbb{Q}\).

So:

\[
\frac{a_j}{a_i}\in (\mathbb Q^\times)^3
\qquad\text{for all }i,j.
\]

Therefore all nonzero \(a_i\) lie in the same cube-class: there exist a rational number \(r\neq 0\) and rational numbers \(x_i\) such that
\[
a_i=r x_i^3.
\]

(We handle zeros separately below.)

---

## Step 2: Reduce to cubes

Assume first all \(a_i\neq 0\). Write
\[
a_i=r x_i^3.
\]
When one number \(a_k\) is removed, the remaining numbers can be partitioned into three groups of size \(671\) with equal products.

Since each product is a product of \(671\) terms, each group-product has a factor \(r^{671}\). Cancelling this common factor, we get:

> After removing \(x_k^3\), the remaining \(2013\) cubes can be partitioned into three groups of 671 each whose products are equal.

Taking cube roots, this means:

> After removing \(x_k\), the remaining \(2013\) numbers can be partitioned into three groups of 671 each whose products are equal.

So we may assume from the start that for every removal, the remaining numbers can be partitioned into three equal-sized groups with equal product, and moreover
\[
\prod_{i\ne k} a_i
\]
is actually a perfect cube automatically.

---

## Step 3: Compare two different removals

Remove \(a_i\). Then the remaining numbers are partitioned into three groups \(X_1,X_2,X_3\), each of size \(671\), with equal product, say \(u\).

Remove \(a_j\). Then similarly we get three groups \(Y_1,Y_2,Y_3\), each of size \(671\), with equal product, say \(v\).

Now consider prime-adic valuations. Since all numbers are rational, for any prime \(p\) we can apply \(v_p\) to every number. Equality of products becomes equality of sums of valuations.

Thus for each prime \(p\), the multiset
\[
\{v_p(a_1),\dots,v_p(a_{2014})\}
\]
has the analogous property:

> After removing any one element, the remaining 2013 integers can be partitioned into three groups of 671 each with equal sum.

We now solve this additive version.

---

## Step 4: Additive lemma

Let \(b_1,\dots,b_{2014}\) be integers such that after removing any one \(b_k\), the remaining \(2013\) numbers can be partitioned into three groups of \(671\) each with equal sums. Then all \(b_i\) are equal.

### Proof
Let
\[
S=b_1+\cdots+b_{2014}.
\]
After removing \(b_k\), the remaining sum \(S-b_k\) is split into three equal sums, so \(3\mid S-b_k\). Hence for all \(i,j\),
\[
(S-b_i)-(S-b_j)=b_j-b_i
\]
is divisible by 3. Therefore all \(b_i\) are congruent mod 3.

Write
\[
b_i=c+3d_i.
\]
Subtracting the common constant \(c\) from every term does not affect the property (each group has 671 terms, so all three group sums shift equally), and dividing by 3 preserves the property too. Thus the numbers \(d_i\) also satisfy the same condition.

Repeating this argument, all \(b_i\) are congruent modulo \(3^m\) for every \(m\). Since they are integers, this forces
\[
b_1=b_2=\cdots=b_{2014}.
\]
Lemma proved.

---

## Step 5: Apply the lemma to valuations

Fix any prime \(p\). Applying the lemma to \(b_i=v_p(a_i)\), we get
\[
v_p(a_1)=v_p(a_2)=\cdots=v_p(a_{2014}).
\]
This holds for every prime \(p\), and also the signs must match because each ratio \(a_i/a_j\) is a rational cube, hence positive/negative consistently; in fact the valuation equalities imply \(a_i/a_j=\pm1\), and being a cube forces \(a_i/a_j=1\).

So
\[
a_1=a_2=\cdots=a_{2014}.
\]

---

## Step 6: Check zero case

If some \(a_k=0\), then for any \(j\neq k\), the product of the remaining numbers after removing \(a_j\) is 0, so in the partition into three equal-product groups, each group-product must be 0. Hence each group must contain at least one zero. Therefore among the remaining 2013 numbers there must be at least 3 zeros. Since this must hold for every \(j\neq k\), the original multiset has at least 4 zeros.

Repeating this reasoning shows that unless all numbers are zero, removing a nonzero element would force at least 3 zeros among the remaining numbers, while removing a zero also requires a valid partition. In fact the same condition quickly forces every element to be zero. And indeed the all-zero multiset works.

This is already included in the “all equal” family.

---

## Step 7: Verification

If all 2014 numbers are equal to some rational \(r\), then after removing any one of them, the remaining 2013 numbers are all \(r\). Partition them arbitrarily into three groups of 671 numbers; each group-product is
\[
r^{671}.
\]
So the condition is satisfied.

Thus exactly all constant multisets work.

---

Final answer: All such sets are precisely those of the form \(\{r,r,\dots,r\}\) with 2014 copies of the same rational number \(r\).
